questions.text: How would you describe [child]'s conversations with others?

options.label: Can talk but finds small talk hard
Prefers not to talk much with others

questions.hint: Preferring less small talk is a style, not a problem by itself.

questions.text: Does [child] have close friends or a consistent social group?

options.label: A little, mostly online or occasional

questions.hint: Online friendships count as real friendships here.

questions.text: Does [child] seem to notice or care about unwritten social rules (e.g., personal space, taking turns in group chats)?
questions.hint: Your best guess is fine — these rules are invisible by definition.

questions.text: Does [child] have a very deep, specific interest they spend a lot of time on?
questions.hint: Deep interests are often a real strength — not just a concern flag.

questions.text: How does [child] react to changes in plans or routine?
questions.hint: Think about unexpected changes, not planned ones.

questions.text: Does [child] have movements or sounds they repeat — like rocking, pacing, hand or finger movements, or repeating words and phrases from shows or conversations?
questions.hint: Many teens keep these private and relax into them at home — what you notice at home counts too.

questions.text: Any strong reactions to sounds, lights, textures, or crowded places?
questions.hint: Pick any that apply — or none.

questions.text: How does [child] cope with stress or frustration?

options.label: Struggles but has some coping strategies

questions.hint: The teen years are stressful for everyone — pick what feels typical.

questions.text: Has [child] mentioned feeling anxious, low, different from peers, or misunderstood?
questions.hint: If comfortable, a short note helps — no pressure to share details.

questions.text: Can [child] independently manage things like their schedule, hygiene routine, or basic responsibilities for their age?
questions.hint: Support needs here are common and useful to know — nothing more.

questions.text: After school or time with people, does [child] seem worn out from the effort of fitting in — needing a lot of alone time to recover?
questions.hint: Needing recharge time is common — we're asking about the effort of fitting in.

questions.text: Does [child] understand jokes, sarcasm, or indirect hints in conversation?
questions.hint: Taking things literally is a communication style, not a failing.

questions.text: Has anyone at school raised concerns about [child]'s learning, focus, or social life?
questions.hint: Feel free to summarize what they said, if you'd like.

questions.text: How has [child]'s sleep been most nights?
questions.hint: Sleep patterns shift a lot in the teen years — pick what feels typical.

questions.text: After time in a loud, bright, or busy place, does [child] need extra downtime or a quiet space to recover, even if they seemed okay at the time?
questions.hint: Many teens push through in the moment and only show the effect afterward, at home.

questions.text: Does [child] seem to not notice pain, cold, or heat the way other people do — like shrugging off a minor injury or not dressing for the weather?
questions.hint: This is about how much pain or temperature registers — not about how tolerant they choose to be.

questions.text: Do [child]'s conversations tend to go both ways — asking questions back, following what the other person says — or does it feel mostly one-sided, about their own topics?

options.label: Mostly one-sided, about their own topics

questions.hint: Some teens learn practiced questions to keep a conversation going — go with what feels genuine, not just polite habit.

questions.text: Can [child] focus on an activity or topic they're really into for an extended stretch of time?
questions.hint: Think about something they've chosen themselves — a hobby, game, or topic — not something assigned to them.

questions.text: Can [child] switch from something they're focused on to something else when you ask, without a lot of pushback?
questions.hint: Many teens hold it together when asked to switch in front of others and only show the frustration afterward, at home — that still counts.

questions.text: Does [child] show or send you things they find interesting — like a video, article, or something they noticed — wanting you to see it too, not because they need something?
questions.hint: This is about wanting to share something because it interests them, not asking for help or approval.
